text: Positive pacing (slowing down over the race) is the most common marathon pacing strategy, observed in approximately 77% of studies examining marathon pacing.
Even pacing in marathons is relatively rare, reported in only about 13% of studies, suggesting most runners fail to maintain consistent splits.
Negative pacing (running the second half faster) is uncommon in marathon racing, observed in approximately 18% of studies.
Gender and race distance are the most substantial factors influencing marathon pacing strategy selection and execution.
population: elite, recreational, and masters marathon runners

text: Marathon pacing strategy is dynamic and influenced by gender, age, performance level, pack running, and physiological and psychological factors.
population: elite, recreational, and masters marathon runners

text: Pacing profiles (the shape of speed over race segments) and pacing ranges (the magnitude of speed variation) are the primary indicators for classifying marathon pacing strategies.
Pack running influences pacing strategy decisions in marathon racing, suggesting drafting groups and competitive dynamics alter individual pacing behavior.
